The Excess Returns model looks at how efficiently a company uses shareholder capital, then compares those returns with the cost of that capital. Instead of focusing on simple earnings multiples, it asks whether each dollar of equity is generating more value than investors require as compensation for risk.

For Ryan Specialty Holdings, the model uses a Book Value of $5.00 per share and a Stable EPS estimate of $5.16 per share, based on weighted future Return on Equity estimates from 4 analysts. The Average Return on Equity is 83.56%, while the Cost of Equity is $0.43 per share. That leaves an Excess Return of $4.73 per share, which is what the model treats as value created above investors' required return.

The Stable Book Value is estimated at $6.17 per share, based on weighted future Book Value estimates from 2 analysts. Feeding these inputs into the Excess Returns framework produces an intrinsic value estimate of about $138.72 per share. Compared with the recent share price of $35.40, this implies the stock is 74.5% undervalued on this basis.

What counts as a "normal" P/E usually reflects how the market views a stock's growth outlook and risk. Higher expected growth or lower perceived risk can support a higher multiple, while lower growth or higher risk tends to point to a lower one.

data.title: Weatherford International plc Just Beat Analyst Forecasts, And Analysts Have Been Updating Their Predictions
data.description:
It's been a good week for Weatherford International plc (NASDAQ:WFRD) shareholders, because the company has just released its latest first-quarter results, and the shares gained 3.7% to US$103. It looks like a credible result overall - although revenues of US$1.2b were what the analysts expected, Weatherford International surprised by delivering a (statutory) profit of US$1.49 per share, an impressive 42% above what was forecast. The analysts typically update their forecasts at each earnings report, and we can judge from their estimates whether their view of the company has changed or if there are any new concerns to be aware of. Readers will be glad to know we've aggregated the latest statutory forecasts to see whether the analysts have changed their mind on Weatherford International after the latest results.

This technology could replace computers: discover the 20 stocks are working to make quantum computing a reality.

After the latest results, the consensus from Weatherford International's eleven analysts is for revenues of US$4.77b in 2026, which would reflect a small 2.2% decline in revenue compared to the last year of performance. Statutory earnings per share are expected to decline 14% to US$5.53 in the same period. Before this earnings report, the analysts had been forecasting revenues of US$4.78b and earnings per share (EPS) of US$5.32 in 2026. The analysts seems to have become more bullish on the business, judging by their new earnings per share estimates.

View our latest analysis for Weatherford International

The consensus price target was unchanged at US$112, implying that the improved earnings outlook is not expected to have a long term impact on value creation for shareholders. There's another way to think about price targets though, and that's to look at the range of price targets put forward by analysts, because a wide range of estimates could suggest a diverse view on possible outcomes for the business. Currently, the most bullish analyst values Weatherford International at US$121 per share, while the most bearish prices it at US$90.00. Still, with such a tight range of estimates, it suggeststhe analysts have a pretty good idea of what they think the company is worth.

Of course, another way to look at these forecasts is to place them into context against the industry itself. We would highlight that revenue is expected to reverse, with a forecast 2.9% annualised decline to the end of 2026. That is a notable change from historical growth of 9.1% over the last five years. Compare this with our data, which suggests that other companies in the same industry are, in aggregate, expected to see their revenue grow 4.8% per year. It's pretty clear that Weatherford International's revenues are expected to perform substantially worse than the wider industry.

Advertisement

The Bottom Line

The most important thing here is that the analysts upgraded their earnings per share estimates, suggesting that there has been a clear increase in optimism towards Weatherford International following these results. Fortunately, the analysts also reconfirmed their revenue estimates, suggesting that it's tracking in line with expectations. Although our data does suggest that Weatherford International's revenue is expected to perform worse than the wider industry. There was no real change to the consensus price target, suggesting that the intrinsic value of the business has not undergone any major changes with the latest estimates.

Keeping that in mind, we still think that the longer term trajectory of the business is much more important for investors to consider. At Simply Wall St, we have a full range of analyst estimates for Weatherford International going out to 2028, and you can see them free on our platform here..

data.title: CGI Uses OpenAI Partnerships And SOK Finance Deployment To Deepen AI Role

  • CGI has also launched multi agent AI in SOK Finance operations, applying automated workflows to financial processes.

CGI (TSX:GIB.A) sits at the intersection of IT services, consulting and managed solutions, where AI driven automation is becoming a core part of client spending. Partnerships with OpenAI and SSAB tie the company more closely to real world use cases in manufacturing and financial services, two areas where clients are looking for productivity gains and more efficient workflows. For investors, these moves help clarify how CGI aims to position its AI offerings inside large, complex enterprises.

The rollout of multi agent AI in SOK Finance gives CGI a live reference case for automated finance operations, which can matter when competing for similar work with other clients. Readers tracking TSX:GIB.A may want to watch how these partnerships convert into additional deployments, new contract wins or extensions, and how clients describe the impact of AI agents on their day to day processes.

That can matter when competing with large IT services peers such as Accenture, Capgemini or IBM for complex, multi-year contracts where clients want partners that already have AI in production, not just on slide decks. data.title: FFO Growth At Essential Properties Realty Trust (EPRT) Tests Long Lease Stability Narrative
data.description:
Essential Properties Realty Trust (EPRT) has kicked off Q1 2026 with total revenue of US$158.8 million, basic EPS of US$0.28 and net income of US$59.8 million, with management and investors also watching FFO of US$114.6 million and FFO per share of US$0.54 as key REIT metrics. Over the past year, the company has seen revenue move from US$129.4 million and basic EPS of US$0.30 in Q1 2025 to US$158.8 million and US$0.28 in the latest quarter, while trailing twelve month revenue reached US$590.7 million and EPS came in at US$1.27. FFO and Revenue Growth Outpace EPS Trend

  • FFO rose from US$92.1 million in Q1 2025 to US$114.6 million in Q1 2026, while total revenue moved from US$129.4 million to US$158.8 million over the same periods, even as basic EPS for the latest quarter sits at US$0.28.
  • Consensus narrative points to stable, inflation-linked cash flows from long leases and service-based tenants, and the numbers back that up in some ways but not others.
    • On the supportive side, trailing 12 month revenue of US$590.7 million and earnings growth of 20.9% suggest the portfolio is still converting rent into profits at scale, in line with the focus on long leases and near full occupancy.
    • On the other hand, the step down in Q1 2026 basic EPS from US$0.30 in Q1 2025 comes alongside a net margin of 43.3% that is slightly below last year’s 44.5%, so profit per share is not moving up as clearly as the FFO and revenue lines.

Consensus viewers who want to see how this earnings mix lines up with longer term storylines can dig deeper into the community angle here 📊 Read the what the Community is saying about Essential Properties Realty Trust.

Margins Stay High While Growth Moderates

  • Over the last 12 months, earnings grew 20.9% while the net profit margin sits at 43.3%, compared with 44.5% in the prior year. This 20.9% earnings growth is below the 5 year average of 24.8% per year in the dataset.
  • Consensus narrative talks about stable, predictable margin growth from inflation protected leases, and the current figures partly echo that story but also show some pressure.
    • High margins in the low 40% range fit with long leases and regular rent bumps that are expected to support steady profitability according to the narrative.
    • However, the small slip from 44.5% to 43.3%, together with forecasts that margins move lower over time, challenges the idea that profitability simply trends higher without any drag from rising expenses or sector specific risks.

  • At a share price of US$31.50, the stock trades on a P/E of 26.6x versus peer REITs at 29.9x and a Global REITs industry average of 15.4x, while a DCF fair value of US$95.49 and an analyst price target of US$37.20 both sit above the current market level. Debt is flagged as not well covered by operating cash flow.

    • Balancing that, the warning that debt is not well covered by operating cash flow and a P/E above the broader Global REITs industry average of 15.4x both point to financial risk and a premium pricing versus many REITs, which sits awkwardly with claims of a straightforwardly strong balance sheet.

Approach 1: Boyd Group Services Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A Discounted Cash Flow, or DCF, model estimates what a company might be worth today by projecting its future cash flows and discounting them back to a single present value figure.

For Boyd Group Services, the latest twelve month Free Cash Flow is about $283.7 million. Analysts provide forecasts for the first few years and, after that, Simply Wall St extrapolates the trend. In this case, the projections suggest Free Cash Flow could reach about $682.4 million in ten years, based on a 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity model that includes ten years of cash flow estimates and a terminal value beyond that.

Putting all of those projected cash flows together and discounting them back to today gives an estimated intrinsic value of about $619.56 per share. Against a current share price of roughly C$162.65, the model points to an implied 73.7% discount, which is a wide gap for investors to think about.

Approach 2: Boyd Group Services Price vs Sales

For companies that are generating revenue, the P/S ratio is a useful way to gauge what the market is paying for each dollar of sales, especially when earnings can be affected by accounting items or short term margin swings.

What counts as a “normal” P/S depends on how quickly investors expect sales to grow and how risky those sales are perceived to be. Higher expected growth and lower perceived risk usually justify a higher multiple, while slower growth or higher risk often go with a lower one.

Boyd Group Services currently trades on a P/S of 1.05x, compared with the Commercial Services industry average of about 1.08x and a peer group average of 2.11x. data.title: It Might Not Be A Great Idea To Buy Minerva S.A. (BVMF:BEEF3) For Its Next Dividend
data.description:
It looks like Minerva S.A. (BVMF:BEEF3) is about to go ex-dividend in the next 4 days. The ex-dividend date is commonly two business days before the record date, which is the cut-off date for shareholders to be present on the company's books to be eligible for a dividend payment. It is important to be aware of the ex-dividend date because any trade on the stock needs to have been settled on or before the record date. This means that investors who purchase Minerva's shares on or after the 29th of April will not receive the dividend, which will be paid on the 13th of May.

The company's upcoming dividend is R$0.0311473 a share, following on from the last 12 months, when the company distributed a total of R$0.33 per share to shareholders. Last year's total dividend payments show that Minerva has a trailing yield of 8.2% on the current share price of R$3.99. Dividends are a major contributor to investment returns for long term holders, but only if the dividend continues to be paid. As a result, readers should always check whether Minerva has been able to grow its dividends, or if the dividend might be cut.

We've found 21 US stocks that are forecast to pay a dividend yield of over 6% next year. See the full list for free.

If a company pays out more in dividends than it earned, then the dividend might become unsustainable - hardly an ideal situation. Minerva reported a loss after tax last year, which means it's paying a dividend despite being unprofitable. While this might be a one-off event, this is unlikely to be sustainable in the long term. Yet cash flow is typically more important than profit for assessing dividend sustainability, so we should always check if the company generated enough cash to afford its dividend. The good news is it paid out just 4.5% of its free cash flow in the last year.

View our latest analysis for Minerva

Click here to see the company's payout ratio, plus analyst estimates of its future dividends.

Advertisement

Have Earnings And Dividends Been Growing?

When earnings decline, dividend companies become much harder to analyse and own safely. If earnings decline and the company is forced to cut its dividend, investors could watch the value of their investment go up in smoke. With that in mind, we're discomforted by Minerva's 9.1% per annum decline in earnings in the past five years. When earnings per share fall, the maximum amount of dividends that can be paid also falls.

Minerva also issued more than 5% of its market cap in new stock during the past year, which we feel is likely to hurt its dividend prospects in the long run. Trying to grow the dividend while issuing large amounts of new shares reminds us of the ancient Greek tale of Sisyphus - perpetually pushing a boulder uphill.

Another key way to measure a company's dividend prospects is by measuring its historical rate of dividend growth. In the last nine years, Minerva has lifted its dividend by approximately 2.8% a year on average.

Final Takeaway

From a dividend perspective, should investors buy or avoid Minerva? It's not a great combination to see a company with earnings in decline and paying out -18% of its profits, which could imply the dividend may be at risk of being cut in the future. However, the cash payout ratio was much lower - good news from a dividend perspective - which makes us wonder why there is such a mis-match between income and cashflow. With the way things are shaping up from a dividend perspective, we'd be inclined to steer clear of Minerva.

With that in mind though, if the poor dividend characteristics of Minerva don't faze you, it's worth being mindful of the risks involved with this business. We've identified 3 warning signs with Minerva (at least 2 which are a bit concerning), and understanding these should be part of your investment process.
